neutral.parent: We honestly never got that far — Periscope came out around the same time and sucked all the wind out of the sails. We pivoted to a communication platform for local newsrooms, enabling producers and editors (i.e., people in the building) to geospatially understand where their crews were and communicate with them more effectively. Local news is a pretty shitty market, though 😅 not awash in cash

Checkout something like Cloudflare R2.

neutral.comment: great question. unlike snapchat maps, we show livestreams directly on the map. we also give users the ability to create communities so that you can actually find what you're looking for on the map discoverability is a big problem in snapchat because you're essentially tapping on random spots on the map and hoping to find what you're looking for.
neutral.parent: This is really neat! I'm just wondering how this is different than Snapchat does already

neutral.comment: imo there has never really been a better time to be a developer :D as a dev, you now have AI tools that you can use to teach yourself any concept you want to learn. as long as you are willing to put in the work. my evenings building this app involved lots of long conversations with ChadGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc. I slowly learned how developers normally tackle the kinds of problems we face with an app like this. i've been an engineer for over a decade now, and I can tell you that the way I learned to code was by doing really small projects and building them out little by little, making them more and more complex over time when you do small projects, you're inevitably going to deal with all of the ugliness and all of the little problems that come with actually shipping real apps. and then you learn how to solve those problems, and you move onto solving bigger problems my stack is fastapi/react native, but you could just as easily use something else so here's my advice to you. pick a small project. get started with it, push it to github, and ask some smart people for feedback. i was constantly bouncing ideas off my partner and off my friends then add to it and think about how you can make it better. if you do this for long enough, you will learn so much than a school or textbook can teach you (although of course those have their place!).
neutral.parent: not the place to ask but im junior dev got inspired by this level of project and expertise any developer want to share good full stack roadmap or resources courses to get good at making something like this

neutral.comment: unlike snapchat, we allow people to create map based communities so you can actually find what you're looking for. we call them layers :D you can invite your own moderators to help you moderate content so you're not just randomly clicking around on the map hoping to find content -- you can find communities with content you're interested in
neutral.parent: so snapchat?

neutral.comment: totally valid concern. in fact this was something that worried us as well. that's why we've created layers - map based communities that can be private / invite only. if you don't want to make your stream public and only available to a community you trust, you can do that too
neutral.parent: It is neat, but consider the privacy and security concerns of individuals whose locations you’re doxxing. We’re in strange times here in the States and personally this would creep me out if I were at a protest and someone was broadcasting who is where to possible bad actors or others with bad intentions.

neutral.comment: We have a few general thoughts on spoofing: Spoofed content is generally fairly easy to verify and discard. If the content posted doesn't match the location/surroundings, it can be identified and removed We allow communities to appoint moderators and remove content which is clearly spoofed We might implement more stringent technical checks on location and IP matching, as well as aggressive banning of users who create spoofed content
neutral.parent: Cool idea! Do you deal with GPS spoofing somehow?

neutral.comment: great question. first, we do allow non-livestream posts direct from your camera so you can create 30 second video clips on the map to help people discover your content and make the app to be useful in a standalone way, we created layer communities to help you group content on the map and invite your friends and share it directly to other platforms we also have a popular feed in the app, which will show your posts to more people if it gets a lot of engagement, helping reward people who make content that sticks
our map does have elevation / terrain built into it, but we don't have anything specifically built for multi story buildings. What use case were you thinking, happy to add it to our big to do list

neutral.comment: so our team consistents of myself and my partner. i handle all the engineering, partner handles product direction / marketing / business / etc and he had the idea of doing a map based app. he's one of the smartest & wisest people ive ever worked with. from the engineering perspective i can say this was the most intense thing ive ever worked on. around 3 yrs of effort, i regularly stayed up until 7am working on it and woke up 930am for my regular engineering job multiple times a week. basically have had not much of a life for several years now but this was highly collaborative - my partner and i would bounce ideas off each other all the time, and refine how we did things. tech stack is python & react native early intuition was that we wanted to crowdsource location data to inform people. we thought it would be nice to go out and be notified about stuff your vicinity. the ideas evolved rather radically from there, into the concept of map-based communities and livestreaming

neutral.parent: I had an interesting idea that included something like this. But it was more along the lines of providing a way for people to organize themselves in order to give what is termed a "common operating picture" in the defense world. Ideally for protestors or citizens in general to help protect themselves and others from mistreatment. I thought of including opt-in real-time location info, map annotations, interactive news graphs to try to get the "truth" out of reporting, messaging/bulletins for orgs to fan out to others. It's not a hard thing technically to achieve. The problems I found when thinking through the problem and solution were more along the lines of no path to monetization, abundant opportunities for misuse by bad actors (organized crime for example), and the govt probably would find a way to categorize this as an intelligence risk and would probably force it down. That and you can already piece a lot of what I planned together with dedicated (but separate) tech channels. Anyway I hope your project works out!

neutral.comment: Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. We appreciate your concern. We both have years of experience working at large Fintech companies, leading multi-tenant, multimillion dollar projects that handled sensitive financial data. Neither of us is particularly experienced with front-end/UX development, which is where most of the vibe-coding came in handy. But we spent a lot of time sweating over the security details of the app, using our combined experience in this area.

neutral.comment: Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. As far as monetization goes, one of our plans to allow users to monetize their content and local expertise. Suppose you make a *This_Earthian* layer. In there you could post a guide to your local attractions, or curated weekly content (could be live, could be short form video). You'd set the layer as a subscription (similar to the Patreon model) and ka-ching! If your content is compelling enough and with a decent sized audience, you can make some moola, and we'd take a small platform fee. There are other commercial aspects, but we are still fleshing those out. Thanks for your question!

Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. I am sure people will find ways, geospoofing is still a concern, but we hope more people will use the app in legitimate ways and help police bad actors. Users can report offensive/graphic posts, and these get blurred out automatically once a reasonable threshold of reports is reached.

neutral.comment: If the goal is truly a public service in the aim of doing good, I would suggest bulletproofing yourself and the site against things that could sway or make you fragile. For example, adding ads means that you are now working for advertisers and won’t want to upset them. There are some really nifty open source libraries out there that can run live streams in almost decentralized fashions. Then, I think you can host the streams for a small amount of time before ejecting them or database dumping them somewhere where other people can torrent them The name of the game is keeping your hosting costs low and renaming anti-fragile in the face of adversity if you’re building a site around free speech and accountability I’m a senior software engineer in California. Happy to help if you’d like

negative.comment: Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. In short, we do not collect any data that is not strictly necessary for the correct functioning of the app. When creating an account, all we ask for is an email and username/password; no gender/birthday/country/etc. When posting, we obviously need location permission to be able to show the location of the event/point of interest. But we do not care about your location otherwise (we do not track continuously). So, in theory, one could remain anonymous if taking the right precautions (we allow signing up with google, so if anonymity is a priority, maybe don't use this method.)

neutral.comment: tysm!! we're a bit different than citizen in that we have a different goal - show people what's happening on the ground and allow them to record anything newsworthy (not just incidents like citizen). we also allow users to record 30 second videos on the map and allow creation of map based communities that ensure that content stays on topic and is geographically relevant

neutral.comment: We honestly never got that far — Periscope came out around the same time and sucked all the wind out of the sails. neutral.comment: Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. The data is pulled straight out of phone camera/microphone. We do not aggregate nor allow uploads from the device’s library. The idea behind Hereabout is for the content to be as raw, unedited, and genuine as possible.
neutral.parent: This reminds me of periscope from back in the day. How is the data pulled and positioned? Does it aggregate other streaming services or is this its own platform?

neutral.comment: Hello! I am OP's partner/co-creator of this app. We have features in the roadmap that would meet this need in a way. Requesting livestreams for money could be a sensitive issue since it borders on “pay-per-surveillance”, which is not something we want to do. If the app continues to gain traction, we want to introduce features that would allow for better organization around events, but right now we have our hands full. Thank you for your suggestion!
neutral.parent: Would it be possible to allow people to send request to go live to users at a specific location? May be a request with small charge out of which you share the revenue with the user who is providing the feed?

neutral.comment: Uuuh. Back in the time when we still had DSLR cameras but they got Geodata and stuff, I thought: if we take the geotag, combine it with focal length and angles from which the photo was taken (most of them had inertia sensors). And if we do this with all the pictures that get uploaded to Google Earth, Maps, Pinterest, etc. Then we could recreate the whole earth in 3D. Google Maps kinda does this a little bit with the option to upload pictures for Street View. But there's a lot more possible.

positive.parent: imo there has never really been a better time to be a developer :D as a dev, you now have AI tools that you can use to teach yourself any concept you want to learn. as long as you are willing to put in the work. my evenings building this app involved lots of long conversations with ChadGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc. I slowly learned how developers normally tackle the kinds of problems we face with an app like this. i've been an engineer for over a decade now, and I can tell you that the way I learned to code was by doing really small projects and building them out little by little, making them more and more complex over time when you do small projects, you're inevitably going to deal with all of the ugliness and all of the little problems that come with actually shipping real apps. and then you learn how to solve those problems, and you move onto solving bigger problems my stack is fastapi/react native, but you could just as easily use something else so here's my advice to you. pick a small project. get started with it, push it to github, and ask some smart people for feedback. i was constantly bouncing ideas off my partner and off my friends then add to it and think about how you can make it better. if you do this for long enough, you will learn so much than a school or textbook can teach you (although of course those have their place!).

neutral.parent: so our team consistents of myself and my partner. i handle all the engineering, partner handles product direction / marketing / business / etc and he had the idea of doing a map based app. he's one of the smartest & wisest people ive ever worked with. from the engineering perspective i can say this was the most intense thing ive ever worked on. around 3 yrs of effort, i regularly stayed up until 7am working on it and woke up 930am for my regular engineering job multiple times a week. basically have had not much of a life for several years now but this was highly collaborative - my partner and i would bounce ideas off each other all the time, and refine how we did things. tech stack is python & react native early intuition was that we wanted to crowdsource location data to inform people. we thought it would be nice to go out and be notified about stuff your vicinity. the ideas evolved rather radically from there, into the concept of map-based communities and livestreaming
